extra.reasoning:
The property requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false. Transition t5 directly breaks this: [t5] on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks>=10): sent := true. It sets sent=true precisely when opted_out is true, contradicting the invariant.

Reachability of t5's guard: ticks starts at 0 and t4 increments it (capped at 10) on each tick. Firing tick 10 times drives ticks from 0 to 10 (0->1->...->10), satisfying ticks>=10. Firing optout once sets opted_out=true (and sent=false). The guard of t5 (opted_out and ticks>=10) is then satisfied, so on the next send_attempt t5 is enabled and sets sent=true.

Counterexample execution (12 events, within the 14-event bound): ten tick events bring ticks to 10; optout sets opted_out=true, sent=false; the final send_attempt fires t5, setting sent=true. The resulting state opted_out=true and sent=true violates globally(opted_out implies not sent).

Note that for send_attempt with opted_out=true and ticks>=10 both t3 (sets sent=false) and t5 (sets sent=true) match; no priority is specified, so an execution in which t5 is chosen is reachable, and the existence of any such violating execution refutes a "every reachable execution" safety property. Thus the property is violated.
